questions.scenario:
Service events show:

10:12:14 service devpolaris-orders-api has started 1 tasks: task 9f4a8c1b
10:12:47 service devpolaris-orders-api registered 1 targets in target-group orders-api-tg
10:13:18 service devpolaris-orders-api has reached a steady state

Target health shows two healthy targets on port 3000. What does this prove?

options.text: ECS started tasks, registered them with the target group, and the ALB accepted their health checks.
Every checkout request and database query is guaranteed correct for the whole release.
The release can skip CloudWatch Logs because target health includes app startup logs.
The task definition no longer matters because steady state replaces the runtime recipe.

questions.explanation: The evidence proves the service path up to target health. That is necessary and encouraging, but it is not proof that every business endpoint is perfect. Logs and smoke tests still add app-level confidence.

questions.scenario:
A new task is RUNNING. Target health says:

Target: 10.0.43.27:3000
State: unhealthy
Reason: Target.ResponseCodeMismatch
Description: Health checks failed with codes: [500]

Startup log says:
release=2026-05-02.5 database=startup_check_failed health=not_ready

What should happen before traffic moves?

options.text: Keep traffic away from the task and fix the readiness or config failure in a new revision or rollback.
Mark the target healthy manually because RUNNING is stronger evidence than health checks.
Stop the old healthy tasks so users are forced onto the new version.
Increase the ALB success-code range to accept 500 for production health.

questions.explanation: RUNNING means the container process exists, but target health decides whether the ALB should trust it. The log explains why readiness failed. Sending traffic anyway would turn a protected failure into a user-facing failure.

questions.scenario:
Deployment status:

old revision 44 running=2 healthy=2
new revision 45 running=2 healthy=0
rollout state: IN_PROGRESS
ALB reason for new targets: Target.Timeout

Why is old/new overlap useful here?

options.text: The old tasks can keep serving while the team diagnoses why the new tasks have not earned traffic.
Overlap means users should send half their requests directly to task IPs.
Overlap means the new revision is automatically successful after a few minutes.
Overlap is only for cost savings and has no availability purpose.

questions.explanation: Rolling deployment overlap buys safety. Old healthy tasks can continue serving while new tasks try to pass health. If the new tasks never earn traffic, the team can stop or roll back without first taking down the known-good revision.

questions.scenario:
A release captain wants evidence that new tasks earned traffic safely:

service event: registered targets in orders-api-tg
target health: 10.0.42.18:3000 healthy, 10.0.43.27:3000 healthy
startup log: release=2026-05-02.5 port=3000 health=ready
smoke test: GET /v1/orders/health-check-order 200

Which evidence belongs in the release note?

options.text: Target health for the new task IPs on the expected port.
Startup logs from the expected release showing config and readiness.
A smoke test or request metric after traffic moved.
Only the fact that the image tag exists in ECR.

questions.explanation: New tasks earn traffic through several checks: service registration, target health, app logs, and a small user-path proof. The image tag is necessary earlier, but it does not prove the service accepted or served the revision.

questions.scenario:
Deployment completed at 10:20. Evidence at 10:23:

rollout state: COMPLETED
target health: 2 healthy
ALB target 5xx: rose from 0.1 percent to 4 percent
app log: release=2026-05-02.5 error=payment_provider_timeout route=POST /v1/orders

What is the best operating response?

options.text: Treat the release as unhealthy at the app level, inspect the changed behavior, and consider rollback to the recorded known-good revision.
Ignore it because target health is the only success signal after deployment.
Delete the target group because healthy targets should not emit 5xx metrics.
Increase desired count immediately because every 5xx spike is solved by more web tasks.

questions.explanation: Target health is a gate, not the whole release verdict. The user path is failing after traffic moved, and the app logs name a dependency symptom. Rollback may be the safest user-protection move while the team investigates.
